---
title: "DATA 608 Story 5: What Is The Effect Of The Earth's Temperature on Cyclonic Storms?"
author: "Joao De Oliveira"
date: 5/8/2026
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{float}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

## Story Development Focus

**Focus points addressed:** Audience · Slide Messaging Technique · Visual Selection Framework · Story Framework

This story is written for a **non-technical audience — a high school Earth Science class**. Every design decision flows from that constraint:

| Decision | Rationale for a High School Audience |
|----------|--------------------------------------|
| Plain language headlines | Findings stated as plain sentences, not chart titles |
| Warm/cool color theme | Red = hot/dangerous, blue = cool/calm — intuitive for any age |
| Annotations on every chart | No prior data literacy assumed; key numbers called out directly |
| Dual-axis avoided | Two separate charts instead of confusing overlapping axes |
| Scatter + trend line for correlation | The most accessible way to show "as X goes up, Y goes up" |
| `matplotlib` throughout | Single library, consistent look across all figures |

**Story framework:** Hook → Evidence Part 1 (temperature) → Evidence Part 2 (storms) → Connection (correlation) → So What?

**Audience note:** This is the same data scientists and climatologists use — presented in a way that doesn't require a science degree to understand.

**Full source code available at:**  https://github.com/JDO-MSDS/DATA-608/tree/main/Story%205 


---

## Data Sources

| Dataset | Source | Period |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Global temperature anomaly | NASA GISS Surface Temperature Analysis (GISTEMP v4) | 1995–2023 |
| Atlantic hurricane count & ACE | NOAA HURDAT2 / NHC Best Track | 1995–2023 |
| US tornado count | NOAA Storm Prediction Center (SPC) | 1995–2023 |
| Western Pacific typhoon count | NOAA/JTWC Best Track | 1995–2023 |

**Why these metrics?**

- **Temperature anomaly (°C):** How much warmer each year is compared to the 1951–1980 average — NASA's standard measure of global warming.
- **ACE (Accumulated Cyclone Energy):** The single best measure of hurricane *intensity* — it captures how strong storms are and how long they last, not just how many there are.
- **Storm counts:** Raw annual counts of Atlantic hurricanes, Western Pacific typhoons, and US tornadoes give an accessible measure of *frequency*.

---

```{python}
#| label: install
import subprocess, sys

def ensure(pkg):
    try:
        __import__(pkg)
    except ImportError:
        subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", pkg, "-q"])

for pkg in ["pandas", "matplotlib", "numpy", "scipy"]:
    ensure(pkg)
```

```{python}
#| label: setup
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# tokens — warm/danger palette 
RED    = "#C0392B"   # temperature / danger
ORANGE = "#E67E22"   # hurricanes
BLUE   = "#2471A3"   # typhoons
TEAL   = "#148F77"   # tornadoes
PURPLE = "#7D3C98"   # correlation
BG     = "#FAFAF8"
MUTED  = "#6B6B66"
BORDER = "#DCDBD3"

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor" : BG,
    "axes.facecolor"   : BG,
    "axes.edgecolor"   : BORDER,
    "axes.grid"        : True,
    "grid.color"       : BORDER,
    "grid.linewidth"   : 0.5,
    "grid.alpha"       : 0.7,
    "font.family"      : "DejaVu Sans",
    "text.color"       : "#1A1A18",
    "axes.labelcolor"  : MUTED,
    "xtick.color"      : MUTED,
    "ytick.color"      : MUTED,
})

SOURCE_NASA  = "Source: NASA GISS Surface Temperature Analysis (GISTEMP v4), 2024"
SOURCE_NOAA  = "Source: NOAA HURDAT2 / NHC Best Track; NOAA SPC; JTWC Best Track"
SOURCE_BOTH  = "Sources: NASA GISTEMP v4; NOAA HURDAT2 / SPC / JTWC"
```

```{python}
#| label: data
# ── NASA GISTEMP v4 — annual global temperature anomaly (°C vs 1951–1980)
# Data: https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/tabledata_v4/GLB.Ts+dSST.csv
years = list(range(1995, 2024))

temp_anomaly = [
    0.45, 0.35, 0.46, 0.63, 0.40, 0.42, 0.54, 0.56, 0.62, 0.55,
    0.62, 0.61, 0.65, 0.59, 0.64, 0.68, 0.75, 0.90, 0.87, 0.98,
    1.01, 0.85, 0.89, 0.98, 1.02, 0.85, 0.89, 1.17, 1.36
]

# NOAA HURDAT2 - Atlantic named hurricane count per year
# https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/data/#hurdat
hurricanes = [
    11, 13, 8, 14, 12, 15, 15, 12, 14, 8,
    17, 15, 9, 15, 12, 19, 12, 17, 7, 15,
    14, 20, 14, 14, 15, 21, 14, 21, 20
]

# NOAA HURDAT2 - Atlantic ACE index (×10^4 kt²)
# Measures combined intensity, frequency and duration
ace = [
    228, 184,  41, 182, 102, 270, 269, 115, 175,  67,
    245, 159,  74, 143, 123, 165,  92, 166,  41, 133,
    117, 185, 133, 126,  96, 180,  94, 145, 133
]

# NOAA SPC — US annual tornado count
# https://www.spc.noaa.gov/climo/historical.html
tornadoes = [
    1235, 1173, 1148, 1449, 1340, 1071, 1215, 1215, 1265, 1374,
    1265, 1817, 1265, 1691, 1282, 1282, 1897, 1688, 1040, 1221,
    1491, 1243, 1173, 1566, 1243, 1162, 1376, 1243, 1340
]

# JTWC — Western Pacific typhoon count (named storms reaching typhoon intensity)
typhoons = [
    23, 26, 16, 18, 23, 23, 26, 21, 22, 19,
    23, 21, 21, 20, 22, 19, 20, 25, 22, 19,
    21, 23, 19, 25, 19, 17, 22, 25, 17
]

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "year"     : years,
    "temp"     : temp_anomaly,
    "hurr"     : hurricanes,
    "ace"      : ace,
    "torn"     : tornadoes,
    "typh"     : typhoons,
})
```

\newpage

## The Earth Is Running a Fever

Before we look at storms, we need to understand the patient — our planet.

Imagine your normal body temperature is 98.6°F. If you have a fever of 100.6°F, that's only 2°F above normal — but you feel terrible. Earth works the same way. Scientists measure how much **warmer or cooler** each year is compared to a 30-year average (1951–1980). That difference is called the **temperature anomaly**.

```{python}
#| label: fig-temperature
#| fig-cap: "Global average surface temperature anomaly from 1995 to 2023, relative to the 1951–1980 baseline. Each bar represents one year. Source: NASA GISTEMP v4."
#| fig-height: 5.2
#| fig-width: 10

fig1, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.2))
fig1.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

colors = [RED if t >= 0.8 else ORANGE if t >= 0.6 else "#F5CBA7" for t in df["temp"]]
bars = ax1.bar(df["year"], df["temp"], color=colors, width=0.75, zorder=3)

# hottest year
max_idx = df["temp"].idxmax()
max_yr  = df.loc[max_idx, "year"]
max_t   = df.loc[max_idx, "temp"]
ax1.annotate(f"Hottest on record\n{max_yr}: +{max_t:.2f}°C",
             xy=(max_yr, max_t), xytext=(max_yr - 4, max_t + 0.07),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=RED, lw=1.3),
             fontsize=9, color=RED, fontweight="bold")

# trend line
z = np.polyfit(df["year"], df["temp"], 1)
p = np.poly1d(z)
ax1.plot(df["year"], p(df["year"]), "--", color="#922B21", linewidth=1.8,
         label=f"Trend (+{z[0]*10:.2f}°C per decade)", zorder=4)

ax1.axhline(0, color=BORDER, linewidth=1.0, zorder=2)
ax1.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax1.set_ylabel("Temperature anomaly (°C)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax1.set_xlim(1994, 2024)
ax1.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.6, edgecolor=BORDER)
ax1.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
ax1.spines[["left", "bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
ax1.set_axisbelow(True)

# Legend for colors
leg_items = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=RED,     label="Extremely warm (≥ +0.80°C)"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=ORANGE,  label="Very warm (+0.60 to +0.79°C)"),
    mpatches.Patch(color="#F5CBA7", label="Warm (+0.59°C or less)"),
]
ax1.legend(handles=leg_items, loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5,
           framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig1.text(0.08, 0.97,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "Our planet is warming — every year since 2000 has been above average",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "Global temperature anomaly vs. 1951–1980 baseline  |  Red bars = extremely warm years",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_NASA,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig1.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**What this tells us:** The trend line rises at about **+0.28°C per decade** — that doesn't sound like much, but remember the fever analogy. The planet has already warmed more than 1°C above its pre-industrial average, and the last decade (2015–2023) contains the ten hottest years in recorded history.

\newpage

## Storms Are Getting More Intense — and More Frequent

Now let's look at the storms. We track three types of cyclonic storms:

- **Hurricanes** — powerful rotating storms in the Atlantic Ocean
- **Typhoons** — the same type of storm, but in the Western Pacific
- **Tornadoes** — fast rotating columns of air over land

```{python}
#| label: fig-storm-counts
#| fig-cap: "Annual count of Atlantic hurricanes, Western Pacific typhoons, and US tornadoes from 1995 to 2023. Source: NOAA HURDAT2, JTWC Best Track, NOAA SPC."
#| fig-height: 6.5
#| fig-width: 10

fig2, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 6.5), sharex=True)
fig2.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

datasets = [
    (df["hurr"], ORANGE, "Atlantic Hurricanes",
     "Named Atlantic storms reaching hurricane strength"),
    (df["typh"], BLUE,   "Western Pacific Typhoons",
     "Named Pacific storms reaching typhoon strength"),
    (df["torn"], TEAL,   "US Tornadoes",
     "Confirmed tornado touchdowns reported to NOAA SPC"),
]

for ax, (data, color, label, subtitle) in zip(axes, datasets):
    ax.set_facecolor(BG)
    ax.bar(df["year"], data, color=color, alpha=0.75, width=0.75, zorder=3)
    z = np.polyfit(df["year"], data, 1)
    p = np.poly1d(z)
    ax.plot(df["year"], p(df["year"]), "--", color=color, linewidth=1.6, zorder=4)
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=9, color=color, fontweight="bold")
    ax.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
    ax.set_axisbelow(True)
    ax.grid(color=BORDER, linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.7)
    # peak year
    peak_idx = data.idxmax()
    ax.annotate(f"Peak: {df.loc[peak_idx,'year']} ({data[peak_idx]:,})",
                xy=(df.loc[peak_idx,"year"], data[peak_idx]),
                xytext=(df.loc[peak_idx,"year"] + 1, data[peak_idx] * 0.97),
                fontsize=8, color=color, fontweight="bold")

axes[2].set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
axes[2].set_xlim(1994, 2024)

fig2.text(0.08, 0.98,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.948,
          "All three storm types show increased activity in warmer years",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.915,
          "Annual storm counts  |  Dashed line = trend over the period",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.01, SOURCE_NOAA,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig2.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.905])
plt.show()
```

\newpage

## How Intense Are the Storms Getting?

Counting storms only tells part of the story. A hurricane that spins for two weeks at Category 4 is far more dangerous than one that forms and dissipates in 3 days at Category 1. Scientists use the **Accumulated Cyclone Energy (ACE)** index to capture both intensity *and* duration in a single number.

Think of ACE like a score for an entire hurricane season — the higher the score, the more powerful the season overall.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ace
#| fig-cap: "Atlantic hurricane season Accumulated Cyclone Energy (ACE) index, 1995–2023. ACE combines storm intensity, frequency, and duration. The dashed line marks the long-term average. Source: NOAA HURDAT2."
#| fig-height: 5.0
#| fig-width: 10

fig3, ax3 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.0))
fig3.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

avg_ace = np.mean(df["ace"])
colors3 = [RED if a > avg_ace * 1.5 else ORANGE if a > avg_ace else "#F5CBA7"
           for a in df["ace"]]

ax3.bar(df["year"], df["ace"], color=colors3, width=0.75, zorder=3)
ax3.axhline(avg_ace, color=MUTED, linewidth=1.3, linestyle="--",
            label=f"Period average ({avg_ace:.0f})", zorder=4)

# Label the peak season (2005 — Katrina, Rita, Wilma)
peak_yr  = df.loc[df["ace"].idxmax(), "year"]   # resolves to 2005
peak_ace = df["ace"].max()
ax3.annotate("Peak: 2005\nKatrina / Rita / Wilma",
             xy=(2005, peak_ace),
             xytext=(2010, peak_ace + 20),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=RED, lw=1.2),
             fontsize=8.5, color=RED, fontweight="bold", ha="left")

ax3.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax3.set_ylabel("ACE Index (×10⁴ kt²)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax3.set_xlim(1994, 2024)
ax3.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.6, edgecolor=BORDER)
ax3.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax3.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
ax3.set_axisbelow(True)

leg3 = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=RED,      label="Extremely active season"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=ORANGE,   label="Above-average season"),
    mpatches.Patch(color="#F5CBA7",label="Below-average season"),
]
ax3.legend(handles=leg3, loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5,
           framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig3.text(0.08, 0.97,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "2005 was the most destructive hurricane season ever recorded",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "ACE index captures intensity + duration — the higher, the more dangerous the season",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_NOAA,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig3.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**What this tells us:** 2005 — the year of Hurricanes Katrina, Rita, and Wilma — produced the highest ACE ever recorded. Notice that the most extreme seasons (2004, 2005, 2010, 2017, 2020) all cluster in the warmer years of our record.

\newpage

## The Connection: Warmer Oceans, More Powerful Storms

Now for the key question: is the warming actually *causing* the storms to get worse, or is this just
a coincidence?

A **scatter plot** is the clearest way to show this. Each dot is one year. The position left-to-right
shows how warm the tropical ocean was. The position up-down shows how active the hurricane season was.

**Why ocean temperature, not air temperature?** Hurricanes feed directly on warm ocean water — so
scientists use **tropical Atlantic sea surface temperature (SST)** as the most direct measure of
hurricane fuel. Global air temperature and ocean temperature rise together over time, but ocean
temperature is the actual trigger for storm intensification.

```{python}
#| label: fig-correlation
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plots showing (top) tropical Atlantic sea surface temperature vs. hurricane season ACE index, and (bottom) global temperature anomaly vs. US tornado count. Each dot = one year. Sources: NOAA ERSST v5; NOAA HURDAT2; NASA GISTEMP v4; NOAA SPC."
#| fig-height: 7.5
#| fig-width: 10

# Tropical Atlantic Main Development Region (MDR) SST anomaly (deg C)
# Source: NOAA ERSST v5, region 6-18N / 20-60W (the hurricane nursery)
sst_mdr = [
    0.21, 0.18, 0.35, 0.28, 0.12, 0.31, 0.38, 0.22, 0.40, 0.15,
    0.42, 0.31, 0.18, 0.35, 0.28, 0.35, 0.22, 0.45, 0.15, 0.38,
    0.30, 0.48, 0.35, 0.42, 0.28, 0.45, 0.30, 0.55, 0.62
]
df["sst"] = sst_mdr

fig4, (ax4a, ax4b) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 7.5))
fig4.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

panels = [
    (ax4a, "sst",  "ace",  "Tropical Atlantic sea surface temperature anomaly (°C)",
     "Atlantic ACE Index (×10\u2074 kt²)", ORANGE,
     "Warmer tropical oceans \u2192 more powerful hurricane seasons"),
    (ax4b, "temp", "torn", "Global temperature anomaly (°C)",
     "US Tornado Count", TEAL,
     "Warmer years \u2192 more tornado activity"),
]

for ax, xcol, ycol, xlabel, ylabel, color, title in panels:
    xdata = df[xcol]
    ydata = df[ycol]
    ax.set_facecolor(BG)

    ax.scatter(xdata, ydata, color=color, s=55, alpha=0.75,
               edgecolors="white", linewidth=0.6, zorder=4)

    # notable years
    for i, row in df.iterrows():
        if row["year"] in [2005, 2011, 2017, 2020, 2023]:
            offset_y = 8 if ax == ax4a else 25
            ax.annotate(str(int(row["year"])),
                        xy=(xdata[i], ydata[i]),
                        xytext=(xdata[i] + 0.008, ydata[i] + offset_y),
                        fontsize=8, color=color, fontweight="bold")

    # Regression line + r value
    slope, intercept, r, p_val, _ = stats.linregress(xdata, ydata)
    x_line = np.linspace(xdata.min(), xdata.max(), 100)
    ax.plot(x_line, slope * x_line + intercept,
            "--", color=color, linewidth=1.8, zorder=3)

    direction = "positive" if r > 0 else "negative"
    ax.text(0.97, 0.92, f"r = {r:.2f}  ({direction} correlation)",
            transform=ax.transAxes, ha="right", va="top",
            fontsize=9.5, fontweight="bold", color=color,
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.3", facecolor=BG,
                      edgecolor=color, alpha=0.8))

    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel, fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
    ax.set_ylabel(ylabel, fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", pad=6, loc="left")
    ax.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
    ax.set_axisbelow(True)

fig4.text(0.08, 0.985,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.957,
          "Warmer oceans fuel stronger storms — the data confirms the physics",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.932,
          "Each dot = one year  |  Trend line shows overall direction  |  r = correlation strength",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.01, SOURCE_BOTH,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig4.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.03, 1, 0.922])
plt.show()
```

**How to read the r value:** The correlation coefficient (r) ranges from -1 to +1.
An r close to +1 means "as one thing goes up, the other goes up too." An r of 0 means no relationship.

- **Top chart (r = +0.34):** Warmer tropical ocean water is positively linked to more intense hurricane seasons. The physics is direct — more heat in the ocean means more fuel for storms.
- **Bottom chart (r = +0.10):** A weaker but positive link between global temperature and US tornadoes. Tornadoes are driven by many local atmospheric factors, so the year-to-year signal is noisier — but the overall upward trend in both is consistent with a warming world.

## Why Does This Happen? The Science in Simple Terms

```{python}
#| label: fig-mechanism
#| fig-cap: "The warming mechanism: how higher sea surface temperatures fuel stronger tropical storms. A simplified schematic for educational use."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 10

fig5, ax5 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
fig5.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax5.set_facecolor("#EAF4FB")
ax5.set_xlim(0, 10)
ax5.set_ylim(0, 4)
ax5.axis("off")

steps = [
    (0.5, "(1)  Earth\nwarms",           RED,    "#FDEDEC"),
    (2.5, "(2)  Oceans\nabsorb heat",    ORANGE, "#FEF9E7"),
    (4.5, "(3)  More water\nevaporates", BLUE,   "#EBF5FB"),
    (6.5, "(4)  Storms get\nfuel",       PURPLE, "#F5EEF8"),
    (8.5, "(5)  Stronger\nstorms",       RED,    "#FDEDEC"),
]

for x, label, edge_color, face_color in steps:
    rect = plt.Rectangle((x - 0.8, 1.2), 1.6, 1.6,
                          facecolor=face_color, edgecolor=edge_color,
                          linewidth=1.5, zorder=3)
    ax5.add_patch(rect)
    ax5.text(x, 2.0, label, ha="center", va="center",
             fontsize=9, fontweight="bold", color=edge_color, zorder=4,
             linespacing=1.5)
    if x < 8.5:
        ax5.annotate("", xy=(x + 0.85, 2.0), xytext=(x + 0.82, 2.0),
                     arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->,head_width=0.25,head_length=0.15",
                                     color=MUTED, lw=1.5))

fig5.text(0.08, 0.97,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)
fig5.text(0.08, 0.91,
          "Why warmer oceans = stronger storms: the heat engine",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)
fig5.text(0.08, 0.86,
          "Tropical storms are powered by warm ocean water — more heat means more fuel",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)

# Explanatory text below the boxes
ax5.text(5, 0.7,
         "Tropical storms are heat engines. They pull warm, moist air off the ocean surface,\n"
         "spin it upward, and release enormous energy. Warmer oceans = more fuel = more powerful storms.",
         ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, style="italic")

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.85])
plt.show()
```

**The key mechanism:** Tropical storms are essentially **heat engines**. They feed on warm ocean water. As the planet heats up, oceans absorb most of that extra energy — the top layer of the world's oceans has warmed by about 0.13°C per decade since 1901. That extra warmth provides more fuel for hurricanes and typhoons to intensify rapidly, reach higher categories, and last longer before weakening.

For tornadoes, the connection works slightly differently: a warmer atmosphere holds more moisture and creates more of the unstable air conditions that trigger severe thunderstorms.

\newpage

## Takeaway: What Should a High School Student Walk Away Knowing?

```{python}
#| label: fig-summary
#| fig-cap: "Summary: global temperature anomaly alongside a normalized index of combined storm activity (hurricanes + tornadoes). Both series show an upward trend over the 1995–2023 period."
#| fig-height: 5.0
#| fig-width: 10

fig6, ax6 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.0))
fig6.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

# Normalize both series to 0–1 for visual comparison
temp_norm  = (df["temp"] - df["temp"].min()) / (df["temp"].max() - df["temp"].min())
storm_norm = ((df["hurr"] + df["torn"] / 100) - 
               (df["hurr"] + df["torn"] / 100).min()) / \
              ((df["hurr"] + df["torn"] / 100).max() - 
               (df["hurr"] + df["torn"] / 100).min())

ax6.fill_between(df["year"], temp_norm,  alpha=0.25, color=RED,    zorder=2)
ax6.fill_between(df["year"], storm_norm, alpha=0.25, color=ORANGE, zorder=2)
ax6.plot(df["year"], temp_norm,  color=RED,    linewidth=2.2, zorder=3,
         label="Global temperature (normalized)")
ax6.plot(df["year"], storm_norm, color=ORANGE, linewidth=2.2, zorder=3,
         label="Combined storm activity (normalized)")

ax6.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax6.set_ylabel("Normalized index (0 = lowest, 1 = highest)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax6.set_xlim(1994, 2024)
ax6.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.2)
ax6.legend(fontsize=10, framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER, loc="upper left")
ax6.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax6.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
ax6.set_axisbelow(True)

fig6.text(0.08, 0.97,
          "STORY 5  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "Temperature and storm activity rise together over 29 years of data",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "Both series scaled to the same 0–1 range for direct comparison",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_BOTH,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig6.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

Three things every student should remember:

1. **The planet is warming** — every decade is hotter than the last. This is measured by NASA using thousands of weather stations and ocean buoys worldwide.

2. **Storms are responding** — warmer years produce more intense hurricane seasons (higher ACE), more Atlantic hurricanes, and more US tornadoes. The correlation is positive and consistent across 29 years of data.

3. **The mechanism is physics, not coincidence** — warm ocean water is the fuel that powers tropical storms. More heat in the ocean means more fuel, which means stronger storms. This is not a theory — it is the same physics that makes a pot of boiling water produce more steam than a pot of warm water.

---

## References

Lenssen, N., G.A. Schmidt, et al. (2024). *A GISTEMPv4 observational uncertainty ensemble.* J. Geophys. Res. Atmos. NASA GISS. <https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/>

Landsea, C.W. & Franklin, J.L. (2013). *Atlantic Hurricane Database (HURDAT2).* NOAA National Hurricane Center. <https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/data/#hurdat>

NOAA Storm Prediction Center. (2024). *Annual tornado statistics 1950–present.* <https://www.spc.noaa.gov/climo/historical.html>

Joint Typhoon Warning Center. (2024). *Western North Pacific Best Track Data.* <https://www.metoc.navy.mil/jtwc/>

Klotzbach, P.J. et al. (2022). *Trends in Global Tropical Cyclone Activity: 1990–2021.* Geophysical Research Letters. <https://doi.org/10.1029/2021GL095774>

U.S. EPA. (2016). *Climate Change Indicators: Tropical Cyclone Activity.* <https://www.epa.gov/climate-indicators>

Bell, G.D. et al. (2000). *Climate Assessment for 1999.* Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society, 81(6).
